edges.description: Remodeling of sinoatrial nodal tissue impairs propagation of pacemaker impulses to the atrial myocardium.
Signaling dysregulation in sinoatrial pacemaker tissue can impair impulse generation or exit to atrial myocardium.
Dropped atrial activation and pauses slow the ventricular rate and can reduce end-organ perfusion.

nodes.description: Recurrent sinus pauses can slow ventricular rate enough to reduce cerebral and systemic perfusion.

evidence.snippet: Clinical symptoms result from the hypoperfusion of end organs.

nodes.description: Degenerative fibrosis and cardiac remodeling of sinoatrial nodal tissue can impair pacemaker-cell automaticity and impulse propagation.

cell_types: cardiac pacemaker cell of sinoatrial node

evidence.snippet: The causes of sinus node dysfunction are intrinsic (e.g., degenerative idiopathic fibrosis, cardiac remodeling) or extrinsic (e.g., medications, metabolic abnormalities) to the sinoatrial node.

nodes.description: Impaired conduction between sinoatrial nodal pacemaker tissue and atrial myocardium produces dropped atrial activation and pauses on the rhythm strip.

cell_types: cardiac pacemaker cell of sinoatrial node

evidence.snippet: Sinus node dysfunction, previously known as sick sinus syndrome, describes disorders related to abnormal conduction and propagation of electrical impulses at the sinoatrial node.

nodes.description: Abnormal intercellular and intracellular signaling, including Hippo, AMPK, mechanical-force, and natriuretic-peptide receptor pathways, can disrupt sinoatrial node automaticity and conduction.

biological_processes: regulation of heart rate by cardiac conduction
cell_types: cardiac pacemaker cell of sinoatrial node

evidence.snippet: Recent studies indicate that SND can be caused by abnormal intercellular and intracellular signaling, various forms of heart failure (HF), and diabetes.
In addition to ion channels, the SAN is susceptible to the influence of various signalings including Hippo, AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), mechanical force, and natriuretic peptide receptors.
